text: Trans-anal Anoscopic Microsurgery was introduced by the Boos Group in the year of nineteen eighty-three. The technique has gained international interest and is currently widely implemented for both benign and malignant lesions, which aren't amenable for colonoscopic resection.

The case we will present is that of a seventy-seven year old male with poor overall health. He has been diagnosed with synchronous locally invasive and metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma and a bulky mid-rectal cancer.

The multidisciplinary team meeting advised on radical radio chemotherapy combined with hormone therapy for the prostate. The patient tolerated the treatment well, with only minor fatigue, mucus discharge and rectal blood loss.
Unfortunately, clear residual disease was found on follow-up endoscopy and MRI. Therefore, a rescue TEM was deemed most appropriate, seeing the overall poor condition.
The patient is positioned prone as the TAM kit is installed. The scope is centralized on the residual cancer tissue, which is visible between the five and ten o'clock positions.
It is advisable to abide by a one centimeter safety margin, even for benign lesions, as has been shown that up to thirty one percent of cases have a focus of associated malignancy.

The surgeon begins dissection with cautery from the lowest portion of the rectal wall, moving upwards to both the left and right sides into the perirectal fatty tissue, carefully avoiding contact with the tumor. A full thickness resection is performed to ensure adequate safety margins.

The surgeon encounters poor tissue quality with noticeable edema and focal fibrosis due to prior radiotherapy. The anterior rectal wall is opened above the peritoneal reflection, revealing adjacent bowel loops. Dissection proceeds cautiously to prevent damage to surrounding intra-abdominal organs.

The surgical team experiences a brief loss of pneumorectum due to perforation, causing the intra-abdominal pressure to equalize and resulting in less optimal exposure of the operation area. This necessitates a more extended, 360-degree resection. The sudden pneumoperitoneum affects the patient's ventilation capacity, which is managed despite his COPD.

The surgeon completes the resection by dividing the remaining strands of tissue, fully excising the tumor. The excision cavity is inspected for hemostasis, and the peritoneal defect is examined to confirm the absence of intra-abdominal trauma.

The surgeon identifies the lumen of the proximal rectum and closes the peritoneal defect using a continuous VLOG-TRIO suture, ensuring no adjacent bowel is incorporated. The rectum is then thoroughly rinsed to minimize tumor seeding and reduce bacterial load.

The surgeon uses multiple V-lock trio sutures to approximate the mucosa, achieving closure and hemostatic control of the 360-degree defect. Careful attention is given to the suturing technique to prevent twisting and stenosis, which would be difficult to correct later.
Although the lumen appeared to be narrowed, it could easily be passed by the scope. The patient was discharged at day five with minimal discomfort and agreeable rectal function.

The pathology showed a YPT-II, R-I, L-I, V-naught, B-I, N-X, M-X, due to suspicion of involved deep margin. At his six months follow-up appointment, endoscopy and MRI revealed some stenosis, without signs of recurrence.

The stenosis, however, was easily passed with a gastroscope, and only a small region of granulation tissue around the rim could still be observed. We plan to follow up the patient closely for at least five years, with during the first year a three-monthly clinical exam, CEA, endoscopy and MRI. Thereafter, a six-monthly review will be advised, with yearly CT chest and abdomen.

In the literature there is mounting evidence for the curative first-line resection of early malignants or even rescue treatment after neoadjuvant treatment for more advanced malignancy. There is also a role for palliative resection in high operative risk patients of patients who have declined curative surgery but look for mere symptom control.
The benefits of them are numerous. as it is a true minimally invasive procedure with low morbidity and mortality, short length of stay and improved organ preservation rate with adequate function.

Nonetheless, this comes at an increased risk of local recurrence and increased need for knee-adjuvant treatment to downsize and downstage compared to rectal resection. Unfortunately, there is occasionally need for an often challenging rescue resection.
To summarize, TEM has shown to be safe and feasible, but occasionally challenging nonetheless. Especially for circumferential, though, and anteriorly located lesions above the peritoneal reflection.
Complications as peritoneal perforation can be managed by TEM, and a three sixty degree resection can be closed and heals with minimal stenosis even after prior radiotherapy.
